text: We're planning these capabilities: Reports & insights, Categories & organization, Core features, Budgets & goals, Settings & preferences, Search & filtering. Which of these matter to you?

options.label: Reports & insights
options.description: Budget Summary Dashboard, Category Breakdown Donut Chart, Spending Insights Period Summary, Daily Spending Chart (Bar/Line Toggle), Month Navigation, Top Navigation Bar, Export CSV, Empty & Error States, Responsive Layout Breakpoints, Reduced Motion Support, Keyboard Navigation & Accessibility
options.rationale: Directly related to what you described.
tradeoffs: + Core to your idea

options.label: Categories & organization
options.description: Recent Expenses List, Expense Detail Popover, Add/Edit Expense Form, Top Categories Breakdown, Biggest Expenses List, Inline Category Creation
options.rationale: Directly related to what you described.
tradeoffs: + Core to your idea

options.label: Core features
options.description: Quick Entry Suggestions, Month-over-Month Comparison, Recurring Expense Templates
options.rationale: Directly related to what you described.
tradeoffs: + Core to your idea

options.label: Budgets & goals
options.description: Settings Dialog, Per-Category Budget Limits
options.rationale: Directly related to what you described.
tradeoffs: + Core to your idea

options.label: Settings & preferences
options.description: Currency Change Toast Notification, Multi-Currency Support
options.rationale: Directly related to what you described.
tradeoffs: + Core to your idea

options.label: Search & filtering
options.description: Search Expenses
options.rationale: Directly related to what you described.
tradeoffs: + Core to your idea

text: How do you want to add expenses — quick entry with just the basics, or detailed with all the info?

options.label: Quick entry
options.description: Just the essentials — id and amount. Fast to enter, minimal effort.
options.rationale: Best when speed matters more than detail.
tradeoffs: + Fast to enter
+ Low friction
- Less detail per expense

options.label: Detailed entry
options.description: Full details — id, amount, description, categoryId, date, paymentMethod, note, createdAt. More info captured per expense.
options.rationale: Best when you want comprehensive records.
tradeoffs: + Rich expense data
+ Better for reports
- More effort per entry

options.label: Flexible
options.description: Start with the basics, optionally add more details like note.
options.rationale: Best of both — quick when you want, detailed when you need.
tradeoffs: + Adapts to the moment
+ No forced fields
- Slightly more complex form

text: Should users be able to create their own spending categories, or is a fixed set of built-in categories enough for you?

options.label: Fixed categories only
options.description: The app ships with 6 preset categories (Food, Transport, Shopping, etc.) and users cannot add, rename, or remove them.
options.rationale: Simpler to use — no setup required, and most personal expenses fit the defaults.
tradeoffs: + Ready to use immediately with no configuration
- Users with unique spending habits (e.g., freelance costs, pet expenses) can't organize their data the way they think about it

options.label: Add custom categories
options.description: Users can create new categories with a name and color, in addition to the 6 defaults. They can also delete categories they don't use.
options.rationale: Covers the most common customization need without overwhelming the interface.
tradeoffs: + Fits a much wider range of personal spending patterns
- Adds a small amount of setup work before the app feels personalized
- Deleting a category raises questions about what happens to expenses already tagged with it

options.label: Fully custom category system
options.description: Users can create, rename, reorder, and delete all categories — including the defaults — and assign custom icons.
options.rationale: Maximum flexibility, but most personal finance users don't need this level of control.
tradeoffs: + Complete control over how spending is organized
- Significantly more setup time before the app is useful
- More complex to build and maintain

text: Should your expense data be saved only on the device you're using, or should it sync across devices so you can access it anywhere?

options.label: Device only (no account needed)
options.description: All expenses are saved in the browser on the current device. No sign-up or login required — open the app and start tracking.
options.rationale: The fastest path to a working app. Ideal if this is a single-device personal tool.
tradeoffs: + Zero friction — no account, no password, works instantly
- Data is lost if the browser is cleared or the device changes
- Can't access expenses from a phone or another computer

options.label: Synced across devices (account required)
options.description: Expenses are saved to the cloud and accessible from any device after logging in. Data is never lost if you switch devices.
options.rationale: Best for users who want to log expenses on their phone and review them on a desktop.
tradeoffs: + Access your data from anywhere, never lose it
- Requires building a login system and backend, which adds significant time and cost
- Users must create an account before they can start

text: Should the app help you re-log expenses you make regularly (like a daily coffee or monthly subscription), and if so, how?

options.label: No shortcuts — manual entry only
options.description: Every expense is entered from scratch each time. Simple and predictable.
options.rationale: Works well if most expenses vary in amount or description.
tradeoffs: + No setup needed, no suggestions to manage
- Logging the same coffee every morning takes the same effort every time

options.label: Smart suggestions based on past entries
options.description: The app notices which expenses you log most often and shows them as one-tap shortcuts at the bottom of the Add Expense screen. No manual setup required.
options.rationale: The most natural way to speed up repeat entries — the app learns from what you actually do.
tradeoffs: + Speeds up daily logging with zero configuration
+ Suggestions improve automatically as you use the app
- Requires enough past data before suggestions appear (new users see nothing)

options.label: Saved recurring templates
options.description: Users manually save templates for recurring expenses (e.g., 'Netflix $15.99 monthly'). Templates can be logged with one tap at any time.
options.rationale: Best for predictable fixed expenses like subscriptions, rent, or gym memberships.
tradeoffs: + Works from day one — no history needed
+ Great for fixed monthly bills
- Requires upfront setup to create each template
- Less useful for variable everyday expenses like meals

text: Do you need to search through past expenses or export your data to a spreadsheet?

options.label: Neither — browse by month only
options.description: Users navigate expenses month by month using the month selector. No search or export.
options.rationale: Keeps the app focused and simple. Works well for most personal tracking needs.
tradeoffs: + Simpler interface with no extra features to explain
- Finding a specific expense from 3 months ago requires clicking through months manually

options.label: Export to CSV only
options.description: A button on the Insights screen lets users download all their expenses as a spreadsheet file for use in Excel or Google Sheets.
options.rationale: Gives power users a way to do their own analysis without adding complexity to the main app.
tradeoffs: + Easy backup and external analysis in familiar tools
+ Simple to add without changing the main interface
- Doesn't help users find a specific past expense quickly

options.label: Search + Export
options.description: A search bar lets users find any expense by merchant name, note, or amount across all months. Plus CSV export.
options.rationale: Most useful once a user has 3+ months of data and needs to find something specific.
tradeoffs: + Quickly find any past expense without browsing month by month
+ Full data portability via export
- Adds UI complexity (search bar placement, results display) that may feel unnecessary early on

text: How much analytical detail do you want in the Insights section — just the basics, or a richer set of charts and comparisons?

options.label: Basic summary only
options.description: Show total spent, daily average, and a simple bar chart of daily spending. Clean and fast to scan.
options.rationale: Covers the core 'how am I doing this month?' question without overwhelming the user.
tradeoffs: + Very easy to read at a glance
- Doesn't show category breakdowns or month-over-month trends

options.label: Full insights dashboard
options.description: Include all charts: daily spending (bar and line toggle), category breakdown with percentages, top 5 biggest expenses, and a this-month vs last-month comparison.
options.rationale: Gives users the full picture of their spending habits, which is the main reward for consistent tracking.
tradeoffs: + Reveals patterns that motivate better spending habits
+ Makes the app feel worth using consistently
- More complex screen that requires a month or two of data to feel meaningful

options.label: Full insights + year-long trend
options.description: Everything in the full dashboard, plus a 12-month rolling chart so users can see their spending trajectory over the whole year.
options.rationale: Powerful for users who track long-term, but overkill for someone just starting out.
tradeoffs: + Shows long-term patterns and seasonal spending shifts
- Requires at least several months of data before it's useful
- Adds more visual complexity to an already information-dense screen
